title: Scope: Global vs Local 🌍
description: Understand where variables live and who can see them.

slides.title: The One-Way Mirror 🪞
slides.content:
# Understanding Scope

When you create a variable using `let` or `const`, it can't always be seen by every line of code in your file.

**Scope** dictates "who can see what". In JavaScript, scope works like a one-way mirror. 

* Inner blocks of code CAN see variables outside of them.
* Outer blocks of code CANNOT see variables created inside inner blocks.

slides.title: Global Scope 🌍
Local Scope (Block Scope) 🏠

quizData.question: Can an 'if' statement block access a variable created in the Global Scope?

options.text: Yes, inner blocks can look OUT and see global variables.
No, if blocks can only use variables made inside them.
Only if using the var keyword

quizData.explanation: Correct! It works like a one-way mirror. The inner blocks look OUT to see global variables. But the global file cannot look IN at the local variables.

slides.title: Scope Clashing 💥
slides.content: What happens if a global and a local variable have the exact same name? The inner scope always wins! Try guessing what this prints before running it.

slides.title: Why Scope is Good 🦸‍♂️
slides.content:
## Preventing Chaos

You might think, "Why not make every variable Global?"

If you have a 10,000 line application and use `let x = 5;` somewhere, you don't want to accidentally overwrite another developer's `let x` miles away!

Local scope functions act like little black boxes. What happens inside them, stays inside them, keeping your large applications safe from cross-contamination! ☣️
